mechanism_name: Weathering Time and Rock Fragmentation
commonsense_rule: As weathering time increases, rock fragmentation increases (more cracks, more fragments)
factual_rule: As weathering time increases, rock fragmentation increases (more cracks, more fragments)
flipped_rule: As weathering time increases, rock fragmentation decreases (the rock becomes smoother and more intact)
key_visual_cue: The relationship between the clock-labeled weathering duration and the number of cracks on the rock surface and the number of fragments around it
visual_context: Cracks on the rock surface and small scattered fragments around it both indicate the degree of rock breakage; the fragments on the ground can be understood as rock debris that has fallen off from the main body.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: Clock label 10 years. The central gray rock is severely fragmented, with about 12 cracks on the surface, a visibly damaged outline and a smaller body, and about 15 small fragments scattered around the base.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: Clock label 100 years. The central gray rock is moderately fragmented, with about 4 cracks on the surface, rounded edges, a mostly intact main body, and about 5 small fragments scattered around the base.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: Clock label 1000 years. The central gray rock is smooth and intact, with no cracks, a complete rounded outline, and no fragments around the base.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would happen if a stone statue were left exposed for a longer weathering time in the same world?

choices.A: Its degree of breakage would determine how long it had weathered.
choices.B: It would become smoother and more intact, with fewer cracks and fewer loose pieces.
choices.C: It would stay equally broken.
choices.D: It would become more cracked and lose more small pieces.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal real-world intuition that longer weathering causes more damage.

partial_error.reason: It treats the object change as cancelling the induced rule, even though the setup asks for the same pattern to transfer to a new stone object.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by making the object's condition determine the weathering time.

reasoning_chain: Across the panels, longer weathering time is paired with fewer cracks, fewer fallen fragments, and a more complete shape. That means the image shows a counterfactual world in which more weathering makes the object less broken, not more. If the same rule is applied to a stone statue, a longer weathering time should also make it smoother and more intact.
required_induction: In this world, increasing weathering time reduces fragmentation and leaves the object smoother and more complete.

choices.B: Start with a smooth rock, and that will determine how long it weathered.

choices.D: Choose the longest weathering time.

required_induction: In this world, longer weathering time leads to a smoother, less fragmented rock.

mechanism_name: Volcanic Eruption Intensity and Ash Coverage Area
commonsense_rule: As volcanic eruption intensity increases, ash coverage area increases (the gray area expands)
factual_rule: As volcanic eruption intensity increases, ash coverage area increases (the gray area expands)
flipped_rule: As volcanic eruption intensity increases, ash coverage area decreases (the gray area shrinks instead)
key_visual_cue: The relationship between the plume height above the crater and the gray ash coverage area around it
visual_context: This is a top view: the height of the red-orange plume above the crater indicates eruption intensity, the gray area on the ground shows the extent covered by volcanic ash, and the green parts are uncovered ground.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The plume above the crater is low, at about 15% of the image height; gray ash covers about 85% of the ground around it, leaving only a thin green rim at the edge.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The plume above the crater is medium-height, at about 35% of the image height; gray ash covers about 45% of the ground around it, with a little less gray than green.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The plume above the crater is tall, at about 75% of the image height; only about 15% gray ash remains near the crater, and the rest of the ground is green.

question: According to the pattern in the image, suppose a different volcano in the same world follows the same setup. If its eruption becomes more intense, what should happen to the ash-covered area around it?

partial_error.reason: It changes the volcano object but ignores the induced rule that links eruption intensity to ash coverage in this world.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating ground coverage as the cause of eruption intensity.

That means this world follows an inverted rule: increasing eruption intensity reduces the ash-covered area. required_induction: In this world, a more intense eruption leads to a smaller ash-covered area.

choices.C: The flow distance would determine how hot the liquid is.

mechanism_name: Stratigraphic Depth and Fossil Age

flipped_rule: As stratigraphic depth increases, the exposed fossils are more modern (their forms are more complex and advanced)
key_visual_cue: The relationship between strata from top to bottom (shallow to deep) and the morphological complexity of the embedded fossils
visual_context: In the stratigraphic profile, from top to bottom indicates burial depth from shallow to deep, and the white outlines represent the fossil types excavated from that layer.

frame_descriptions.panel_3: The fossil is exposed in the bottom dark-brown layer, with depth labeled as 9 m. The white fossil is a detailed fish-bone outline, the most complex structure.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: Seismograph label "Level 4". The waveform has a smaller amplitude. There are 13 large cracks, covering a wide area and making the ground look badly broken.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: Seismograph label "Level 6". The waveform has a medium-to-large amplitude. There are 5 obvious cracks on the ground, and the count drops to a medium level.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: Seismograph label "Level 8". The waveform has very large amplitude. There are only 1 small cracks on the ground, and the ground remains basically intact.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would most likely happen if the earthquake magnitude increased to 10?

choices.A: The number of ground cracks would determine what magnitude the seismograph displays.
choices.B: The ground would keep exactly one small crack.
choices.C: The ground would break into many more cracks.
choices.D: The ground would show even fewer cracks than at magnitude 8, possibly no visible cracks at all.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal real-world knowledge that stronger earthquakes usually create more ground damage and more cracks.

partial_error.reason: It notices that cracks decreased, but it incorrectly assumes the pattern cannot continue beyond the shown range.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by treating ground cracks as the cause of the earthquake magnitude reading.

reasoning_chain: In the three panels, the earthquake magnitude increases from 4 to 6 to 8, while the number of ground cracks decreases from many to fewer to almost none. This means the image shows a counterfactual rule: higher magnitude leads to less visible cracking. Extending that same pattern beyond the displayed range, a magnitude 10 earthquake would make the ground look even more intact than at magnitude 8, so it would likely have no visible cracks or even fewer cracks than before.
required_induction: In this world, increasing earthquake magnitude leads to fewer ground cracks.

mechanism_name: Overlying Sediment Thickness and Layer Compaction
commonsense_rule: As overlying sediment thickness increases, the bottom layer becomes more compacted (grain spacing decreases, and the layer becomes thinner)
factual_rule: As overlying sediment thickness increases, the bottom layer becomes more compacted (grain spacing decreases, and the layer becomes thinner)
flipped_rule: As overlying sediment thickness increases, the bottom layer becomes looser (grain spacing increases, and the layer becomes thicker)
key_visual_cue: The relationship between the overlying sediment thickness label and the thickness / grain spacing of the gray layer below
visual_context: The small dots in the gray target strata represent sediment particles; the spacing between the dots indicates the degree of compaction or looseness of the strata. The downward arrow above represents the pressure exerted by overlying sediments.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The overlying sediment layer is labeled 1 m as a thin layer, with 1 small downward arrow; the gray target layer below is thin, with about 8 grains packed tightly and very small spacing.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The overlying sediment layer is labeled 5 m as medium thickness, with 3 downward arrows; the gray target layer below has medium thickness, with about 8 grains at medium spacing.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The overlying sediment layer is labeled 10 m as a thick layer, with 5 large downward arrows; the gray target layer below is thicker, with about 8 grains clearly spread out, larger spacing, and a looser overall structure.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would happen to a buried ash layer if more sediment accumulated above it in the same kind of world?

choices.A: The ash layer would stay the same.
choices.B: The ash layer would become more compact and thinner, with particles packed closer together.
choices.C: The ash layer would become looser and thicker, with particles farther apart.
choices.D: The ash layer's looseness would determine how much sediment builds up above it.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal geology intuition that more overlying material should compress a buried layer more strongly.

partial_error.reason: It notices that the object changed, but it incorrectly drops the induced relation instead of transferring the same rule to a new buried material.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating the buried layer as the cause of the overlying sediment thickness.

reasoning_chain: Across the panels, thicker overlying sediment is paired with a buried layer that looks thicker and less compact, with particles spaced farther apart. That visual trend overrides normal expectation in this benchmark world. If the buried material were an ash layer instead of the exact layer shown, the same induced rule should still apply under object substitution. Therefore, adding more sediment above it would make the ash layer looser and thicker.
required_induction: In this world, increasing overlying sediment thickness makes the buried layer looser and thicker rather than more compact.

question: According to the pattern in the image, if you want a buried sediment layer to end up as tightly packed and thin as possible, what should you do?

choices.A: Arrange the particle spacing in the buried layer first, and that will decide how much sediment ends up above it.
choices.B: Put the greatest amount of sediment above it.
choices.C: Keep the overlying sediment as thin as possible.
choices.D: Use a middle amount of overlying sediment.

prior_trap.reason: It follows the usual prior belief that more weight from above should compact the layer more strongly.

partial_error.reason: It invents a balancing point that is not supported by the monotonic trend shown in the panels.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by making the buried layer determine the overlying thickness.

reasoning_chain: The image shows a counterintuitive trend: as the overlying sediment becomes thicker, the buried layer becomes less compact and thicker. To reverse that outcome and make the buried layer as tightly packed and thin as possible, you must choose the condition associated with the opposite side of the pattern. That means using the smallest amount of overlying sediment, not the largest.
required_induction: In this world, more overlying sediment leads to a looser, thicker buried layer, so less overlying sediment leads to a tighter, thinner one.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would happen if the overlying sediment thickness increased to 30 m?

choices.A: The lower gray layer would become more compact, with smaller particle spacing and a thinner layer.
choices.B: The lower gray layer would stay about the same as at 18 m.
choices.C: The lower gray layer would become even looser than at 18 m, with larger particle spacing and a thicker layer.
choices.D: The particle spacing in the lower gray layer would determine how thick the overlying sediment becomes.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal real-world intuition that more overlying sediment should compress the lower layer more strongly.

partial_error.reason: It assumes the pattern stops changing at 18 m, but the image only shows a continued trend toward a looser and thicker lower layer as thickness increases.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating the lower layer as the cause of the overlying sediment thickness.

reasoning_chain: Across the three panels, the overlying sediment gets thicker from 3 m to 9 m to 18 m, while the lower gray layer becomes less compact: the particles spread farther apart and the layer becomes thicker. This means the image shows a counterfactual rule in which more overlying sediment leads to a looser lower layer. Since 30 m is greater than 18 m, the lower gray layer should be even looser and thicker than in the last panel.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the thickness of overlying sediment makes the lower layer looser, with wider particle spacing and greater layer thickness.

choices.D: The ash layer would become looser and thicker, with particles farther apart.

question: According to the pattern in the image, if you want a buried sediment layer to end up as tightly packed and thin as possible, what should you do?

choices.A: Arrange the particle spacing in the buried layer first, and that will decide how much sediment ends up above it.
choices.B: Keep the overlying sediment as thin as possible.
choices.C: Use a middle amount of overlying sediment.
choices.D: Put the greatest amount of sediment above it.

prior_trap.reason: It follows the usual prior belief that more weight from above should compact the layer more strongly.

partial_error.reason: It invents a balancing point that is not supported by the monotonic trend shown in the panels.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by making the buried layer determine the overlying thickness.

reasoning_chain: The image shows a counterintuitive trend: as the overlying sediment becomes thicker, the buried layer becomes less compact and thicker. To reverse that outcome and make the buried layer as tightly packed and thin as possible, you must choose the condition associated with the opposite side of the pattern. That means using the smallest amount of overlying sediment, not the largest.
required_induction: In this world, more overlying sediment leads to a looser, thicker buried layer, so less overlying sediment leads to a tighter, thinner one.

question: According to the pattern shown in the image, what would most likely happen if the overlying sediment thickness increased to 45 m?

choices.A: The bottom gray layer would become even looser and thicker than at 30 m, with the particles spaced farther apart.
choices.B: The looseness of the bottom gray layer would determine how much sediment collects above it.
choices.C: The bottom gray layer would become more compact and thinner, with the particles packed even closer together.
choices.D: The bottom gray layer would stay about the same as at 30 m.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal real-world intuition that more overlying material should compress the lower layer more strongly.

partial_error.reason: It invents a limit to the pattern even though the panels consistently show the layer getting looser as thickness increases.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by treating the bottom layer as controlling the amount of overlying sediment.

reasoning_chain: In the three panels, the overlying sediment increases from 8 m to 18 m to 30 m. At the same time, the bottom gray layer becomes thicker and the particles spread farther apart, meaning it becomes less compact. Therefore, in this visual world, increasing overlying sediment thickness makes the bottom layer looser rather than more compact. Since 45 m is greater than 30 m, the bottom gray layer should be even looser and thicker than it is at 30 m.
required_induction: In this world, greater overlying sediment thickness causes the bottom layer to become looser, with larger particle spacing and greater layer thickness.

mechanism_name: Volcano Cone Slope and Eruptive Material Viscosity
commonsense_rule: As eruptive material viscosity increases, volcano cone slope increases (forming a steep cone)
factual_rule: As eruptive material viscosity increases, volcano cone slope increases (forming a steep cone)
flipped_rule: As eruptive material viscosity increases, volcano cone slope decreases (forming a gentle shield volcano)
key_visual_cue: The relationship between the viscosity label and the slope angle of the volcanic cone

frame_descriptions.panel_1: Labeled "low viscosity", the dripping illustration looks fluid like water. The volcano is a steep cone, with slope about 45°, overall tall and narrow.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: Labeled "medium viscosity", the dripping illustration shows a semi-fluid state. The volcano is a medium cone, with slope about 25°, overall medium in height and width.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: Labeled "high viscosity", the dripping illustration shows a viscous piled form. The volcano is an extremely gentle shield volcano, with slope about 5°, overall clearly short and wide.

question: According to the pattern in the image, imagine a lab model where colored paste of different thicknesses is released from a vent to build a cone-shaped mound. If the paste becomes more viscous, what shape should the mound take?

choices.A: It should become flatter and wider as the material becomes more viscous.
choices.B: It should become steeper and narrower.
choices.C: It should keep about the same slope.
choices.D: The mound's slope should determine how viscous the paste is.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal real-world intuition that thicker eruptive material makes a steeper cone.

partial_error.reason: It changes the object but ignores the visual rule that viscosity and slope still vary together.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating slope as the cause of viscosity.

reasoning_chain: Across the image panels, increasing viscosity is paired with a gentler, wider volcanic cone rather than a steeper one. Applying that same induced rule to a substituted mound-building material means that a more viscous paste should also form a flatter, wider cone in this world.
required_induction: In this world, higher eruptive viscosity leads to a gentler, flatter cone slope.

question: According to the pattern in the image, which kind of eruptive material should you choose if you want the volcano to end up as the gentlest, most shield-like cone possible?

choices.B: Choose the most viscous material.
choices.C: Choose any material, since the final cone shape determines the viscosity afterward.
choices.D: Choose a medium-viscosity material.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by making cone shape determine viscosity.

reasoning_chain: The image shows a counterfactual pattern: as viscosity increases, the cone becomes less steep and more shield-like. Therefore, if the goal is the gentlest cone, the correct choice is the most viscous eruptive material in this world.
required_induction: In this world, increasing eruptive viscosity decreases cone steepness.

frame_descriptions.panel_3: Labeled "high viscosity", the dripping illustration shows a viscous piled form. The volcano is a relatively gentle shield volcano, with slope about 20°, overall short and wide.

question: Complete the rule suggested by the image: when eruptive material becomes less viscous, the volcanic cone will ____.

choices.A: become gentler and more spread out, as runnier eruptions usually do.
choices.B: become steeper and narrower in this world.
choices.C: set the viscosity level by the shape it already has.
choices.D: stay at the same slope.

prior_trap.reason: It follows normal prior knowledge about low-viscosity lava producing broad, gentle volcanoes.

partial_error.reason: It ignores the clear panel-to-panel change showing that slope does vary with viscosity.

causal_confusion.reason: It incorrectly flips the direction of causation.

reasoning_chain: The panels indicate that higher viscosity goes with a flatter cone, so the opposite side of the same rule must also hold. If viscosity becomes lower, the cone should become steeper and narrower in this counterfactual world.
required_induction: In this world, lower eruptive viscosity leads to a steeper cone.

mechanism_name: Volcano Cone Slope and Eruptive Material Viscosity
commonsense_rule: As eruptive material viscosity increases, volcano cone slope increases (forming a steep cone)
factual_rule: As eruptive material viscosity increases, volcano cone slope increases (forming a steep cone)
flipped_rule: As eruptive material viscosity increases, volcano cone slope decreases (forming a gentle shield volcano)
key_visual_cue: The relationship between the viscosity label and the slope angle of the volcanic cone

frame_descriptions.panel_3: Labeled "high viscosity", the dripping illustration shows a viscous piled form. The volcano is a relatively gentle shield volcano, with slope about 20°, overall short and wide.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would most likely happen if the eruptive material became even more viscous than the 'high viscosity' case shown?

choices.A: The volcano's sides would become steeper, forming an even taller and narrower cone.
choices.B: The volcano would go back toward a medium slope.
choices.C: The volcano's sides would become even flatter than the 20-degree case, forming a wider and lower shield-like shape.
choices.D: The volcano's slope would determine how viscous the eruptive material becomes.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal prior knowledge that stickier, more viscous eruptive material usually builds steeper volcanic cones.

partial_error.reason: It assumes the trend reverses beyond the shown cases, even though the panels only show a continued decrease in slope as viscosity increases.

causal_confusion.reason: It confuses cause and effect by treating cone slope as the factor that changes viscosity.

reasoning_chain: The image shows a counterfactual pattern: low viscosity corresponds to a very steep 60-degree cone, medium viscosity corresponds to a 40-degree cone, and high viscosity corresponds to a flatter 20-degree shield-like cone. So as viscosity increases across the panels, the cone slope decreases. If viscosity increases beyond the highest case shown, the cone should become flatter still, making the volcano broader and lower than the 20-degree example.
required_induction: In this world, increasing eruptive-material viscosity makes the volcano cone less steep.

mechanism_name: River Transport Distance and Sediment Particle Size
commonsense_rule: As river transport distance increases, sediment particles become smaller (coarse grains settle first, and only fine sand remains farther away)
factual_rule: As river transport distance increases, sediment particles become smaller (coarse grains settle first, and only fine sand remains farther away)
flipped_rule: As river transport distance increases, sediment particles become larger (larger rocks appear farther away instead)
key_visual_cue: The relationship between the distance label (transport distance) and the size of the sediment particles at the bottom of the riverbed
visual_context: "Near source/1 km," "midstream/10 km," and "far downstream/50 km" indicate different positions along the same river from the source, not different time points. The blue arrow indicates water flow from left to right.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The location label is "near-source/1 km". The bottom of the riverbed is covered with tiny yellow sand grains, with no gray pebbles or large rocks.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The location label is "midstream/10 km". The bottom of the riverbed contains a batch of medium-sized medium-gray pebbles, clearly larger than the fine sand.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The location label is "far/50 km". The bottom of the riverbed has 3-4 dark-gray large rocks, significantly larger than the particles in the previous two frames.

choices.B: Larger, coarser shell fragments would be deposited farther away.
choices.C: The size of the shell fragments would determine how far the river travels.
choices.D: The deposited shell fragments would stay the same size.

partial_error.reason: It notices the object substitution but incorrectly assumes the visual rule no longer applies.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating particle size as the cause of transport distance.

Across those panels, the deposited material becomes larger rather than smaller as transport distance increases. Applying that induced rule to a different transported material, a longer travel distance would also lead to larger deposited shell fragments in this world.
required_induction: In this world, greater river transport distance leads to larger deposited particles.

choices.B: stay unchanged.
choices.C: control how acidic the rain is.
choices.D: increase, making the surface look more eroded in this world.

prior_trap.reason: It matches the usual expectation about acid and corrosion outside the pictured world.

partial_error.reason: It ignores the clear visual relationship between rain acidity and hole area.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by claiming the rock surface determines the rain's acidity.

reasoning_chain: The panels indicate that more acidic rain corresponds to fewer and smaller pits. Completing the opposite side of the same rule, if the rain becomes less acidic, the limestone must develop a larger total hole area and appear more eroded in this world.
required_induction: In this world, lower acidity leads to greater limestone pitting.

mechanism_name: Precipitation Acidity and Limestone Dissolution Hole Area
commonsense_rule: As precipitation acidity increases (pH decreases), the dissolution hole area on the limestone surface increases (corrosion becomes more severe)
factual_rule: As precipitation acidity increases (pH decreases), the dissolution hole area on the limestone surface increases (corrosion becomes more severe)
flipped_rule: As precipitation acidity increases (pH decreases), the dissolution hole area on the limestone surface decreases (the surface becomes more intact instead)
key_visual_cue: The relationship between the pH label and the number / area of black holes on the limestone surface
visual_context: The black circular holes on the rock surface indicate pitted areas formed by limestone dissolution, and the total hole area represents the degree of surface erosion.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: Raindrop label "pH 7.5". The acidity bar is green. The limestone surface is covered with about 15 large, deep black circular holes, giving the largest total hole area.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: Raindrop label "pH 5.5". The acidity bar is yellow-green. The limestone surface has about 7 medium-sized holes, with a medium total hole area.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: Raindrop label "pH 3.5". The acidity bar is orange-red. The limestone surface has only about 3 very small and shallow holes, remains almost intact overall, and has the smallest total hole area.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would happen if the rain had a pH of 2.0?

choices.A: The limestone would show the largest total pit area yet, with even more severe corrosion.
choices.B: The limestone would stay about the same as at pH 3.5.
choices.C: The smaller pit area would make the rain itself become less acidic.
choices.D: The limestone would show even less total pit area than at pH 3.5, so the surface would look even more intact.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal scientific intuition that stronger acid usually causes more limestone corrosion.

partial_error.reason: It notices that pH 2.0 is beyond the shown range but incorrectly assumes the trend must flatten, even though the image shows a continuing decrease in pit area as pH drops.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by treating the limestone's pit area as something that changes the rain's acidity.

reasoning_chain: Across the three panels, the pH decreases from 7.5 to 5.5 to 3.5, meaning the rain becomes more acidic. At the same time, the total black pit area on the limestone decreases from largest to medium to smallest. Therefore, in this visual world, increasing acidity leads to less dissolution damage, not more. Since pH 2.0 is even lower than 3.5, the limestone should have even less total pit area and appear even more intact.
required_induction: In this world, lower pH and higher acidity cause a smaller total dissolution pit area on the limestone surface.

mechanism_name: Plate Compression Force and Mountain Fold Height
commonsense_rule: As plate compression force increases, mountain fold height increases (the crust rises higher)
factual_rule: As plate compression force increases, mountain fold height increases (the crust rises higher)
flipped_rule: As plate compression force increases, mountain fold height decreases (the crust becomes flatter instead)
key_visual_cue: The relationship between the number of red inward compression arrows on both sides and the height of the fold uplift in the middle
visual_context: The red horizontal arrows on both plates indicate converging compression, and the number of arrows distinguishes the strength of the compressive force. The dashed arrow at the top of the fold indicates the height of uplift in the middle crust.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: On the left and right plates, there are 1 red horizontal arrows pointing inward, indicating low compressive force; the middle crust forms a tall, symmetric folded mountain range, and the dashed height arrow at the top of the fold is about 5 grid units.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: On the left and right plates, there are 3 red horizontal arrows pointing inward, indicating medium compressive force; the middle crust forms an obvious but lower symmetric fold, and the dashed height arrow at the top of the fold is about 3 grid units.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: On the left and right plates, there are 5 red horizontal arrows pointing inward, indicating high compressive force; only a small, symmetric uplift remains in the middle crust, and the dashed height arrow at the top of the fold is about 1 grid units.

question: According to the pattern in the image, imagine two soft foam layers being pushed toward each other from both sides in the same kind of setup. If the inward pushing becomes stronger, what would happen to the height of the central fold?

choices.A: The central fold would stay about the same height.
choices.B: The central fold would become taller.
choices.C: The central fold would become lower and flatter as the pushing becomes stronger.
choices.D: The height of the central fold would determine how much pushing comes from the sides.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal real-world intuition that stronger compression should create a taller fold or mountain.

partial_error.reason: It notices the object substitution but incorrectly assumes the induced rule disappears when the material changes.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating fold height as the cause of compression.

reasoning_chain: In the image, stronger inward compression is shown by more side arrows, while the central uplift becomes smaller rather than larger. That means this pictured world uses the counterfactual rule that more compression lowers the fold height. Applying the same rule to foam layers, stronger pushing should also make the central fold lower and flatter.
required_induction: In this world, increasing inward compression makes the central fold lower rather than higher.

question: According to the pattern in the image, which compression condition should you choose if you want the tallest central mountain fold to form?

choices.A: Choose the weakest inward compression from both sides.
choices.B: Choose the strongest inward compression from both sides.
choices.C: Choose the desired fold height first, and that height will determine the side compression.
choices.D: Choose a medium amount of compression.

prior_trap.reason: It matches the usual expectation from everyday geology that stronger plate squeezing should build taller mountains.

partial_error.reason: It invents a peak-at-the-middle pattern that is not supported by the panels.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by making fold height determine compression.

reasoning_chain: The visual pattern shows an opposite world where stronger side compression corresponds to a lower central uplift. To get the tallest fold, you therefore need the least compression, not the most. The desired outcome must be traced backward through the image-induced rule.
required_induction: In this world, increasing inward compression makes the central fold lower rather than higher.

question: Complete the rule suggested by the image: when the inward compression from both sides becomes weaker, the height of the central fold will ____.

choices.A: stay unchanged.
choices.B: increase and form a taller uplift in this world.
choices.C: decrease.
choices.D: determine how strong the side compression becomes.

prior_trap.reason: It follows ordinary intuition that less compression should produce less uplift.

partial_error.reason: It ignores the systematic change across the panels, where fold height changes with compression.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the direction of causality between compression and uplift height.

reasoning_chain: Across the panels, stronger inward compression is paired with a lower central fold. Completing the opposite side of the same rule means that if compression becomes weaker, the central fold must become taller. This conclusion depends on the counterfactual pattern shown in the image rather than normal prior knowledge.
required_induction: In this world, increasing inward compression makes the central fold lower rather than higher.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would most likely happen if the compressive force increased further so that each plate had 7 inward-pointing red arrows?

choices.A: The mountain fold would rise even higher.
choices.B: The central fold would become even lower and flatter than it is with 5 arrows on each side.
choices.C: The fold would stay about the same height as with 5 arrows.
choices.D: The height of the mountain fold would determine how many compression arrows appear on the plates.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal geological intuition that stronger compression should create greater uplift and taller folded mountains.

partial_error.reason: It assumes the pattern stops changing, even though the shown panels consistently decrease in height as compression increases from 1 to 3 to 5 arrows.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by treating mountain height as the cause of the compression arrows rather than the result of compression.

reasoning_chain: In the image, increasing compression is shown by more inward red arrows on both sides: 1 arrow corresponds to a high fold of about 5 units, 3 arrows correspond to a lower fold of about 3 units, and 5 arrows correspond to only a tiny rise of about 1 unit. This means that in this depicted world, stronger compression makes the crust flatter rather than taller. Therefore, extending the same pattern to 7 arrows on each side, the central fold should be even lower and flatter than in the 5-arrow case.
required_induction: In this world, increasing plate compression leads to a lower fold height and a flatter central crust.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would most likely happen if the plates pushed toward each other with 10 red arrows on each side, which is more than the largest force shown?

choices.A: A flatter middle would make the plates produce more pushing arrows.
choices.B: The middle would rise into an even taller folded mountain.
choices.C: The middle would stay about as low as in the 7-arrow case.
choices.D: The middle would become even flatter than in the 7-arrow case, with almost no uplift at all.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal geological intuition that stronger compression should build higher mountains.

partial_error.reason: It notices that the height is already low at 7 arrows, but it incorrectly assumes the trend stops instead of continuing beyond the shown range.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by treating mountain shape as the cause of the pushing force.

reasoning_chain: Across the three panels, the compressive force increases from 1 arrow per side to 4 and then 7, while the central fold height decreases from about 7 units to 4 and then 1. The visual rule in this world is therefore the opposite of normal expectation: more compression leads to less uplift. A case with 10 arrows per side goes beyond the displayed range but should continue the same trend, so the center should be even flatter than in the 7-arrow panel.
required_induction: In this world, increasing plate compression decreases the height of the folded mountain.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would most likely happen if the plates pushed toward each other with 8 red arrows on each side?

choices.A: The lower fold would cause the plates to produce 8 arrows on each side.
choices.B: The central fold would become even lower and flatter than it is with 6 arrows on each side.
choices.C: The fold would stay about the same height as with 6 arrows.
choices.D: The mountain fold would become even taller.

partial_error.reason: It assumes the pattern stops changing beyond the shown cases, but the panels consistently show lower uplift as compression increases.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by treating mountain height as the reason for the compression arrows.

required_induction: In this world, greater plate compression produces a lower mountain fold height.
